macro_summary: Markets return from the Juneteenth holiday into a hawkish macro backdrop: the June 17 FOMC under new Chair Kevin Warsh held rates at 3.50%–3.75% unanimously but the dot plot flipped decisively — the 2026 median projection rose to 3.8%, implying at least one hike, with 9 of 18 officials penciling in a hike and PCE inflation revised sharply higher to 3.6% for 2026. Partially offsetting this, a US-Iran memorandum of understanding signed June 15 reopened the Strait of Hormuz, sending oil prices down ~5% to ~$80–83/bbl — though analysts caution the geopolitical risk premium removal has already been largely priced in. Today (June 22) carries no major scheduled data or earnings; the week's weight sits squarely on Tuesday's PMIs, Wednesday's Micron earnings and Fed stress test results (June 24), and Thursday's May PCE and final Q1 GDP print — making this a positioning week rather than a reaction week. S&P 500 futures are hovering around 7,550, recovering from the post-FOMC selloff.
key_events: Fed held rates at 3.50%–3.75% on June 17 (12-0 vote); dot plot median rose to 3.8% for 2026, implying ≥1 hike — first explicitly hawkish flip since rate-cut cycle began
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh removed forward guidance, declined to submit a dot, and announced 5 policy task forces; traders now price a hike as early as October 2026
US-Iran MOU signed June 15 to end 15-week conflict and reopen Strait of Hormuz; WTI fell ~5% to ~$80/bbl but remains 40% above year-start; full supply normalization expected to take months
Trump announced June 18 (via Truth Social) that Apple agreed to work with Intel on US chip manufacturing; INTC surged ~10.6% on the day but neither company issued official confirmation
FedEx completed FDXF freight spin-off June 1; FDX reports Q4 FY2026 earnings Tuesday June 23
Micron (MU) reports fiscal Q3 2026 earnings Wednesday June 24 after close — widely seen as the week's highest-stakes print
Fed releases 2026 bank stress test results Wednesday June 24 at 4pm EDT (32 banks; results will NOT affect capital requirements in 2026, per the Fed's own pre-announcement)
May PCE price index and final Q1 GDP estimate due Thursday June 25 — first post-FOMC inflation read; April PCE already ran at 3.80% YoY headline / 3.30% core

ideas.name: Micron Technology, Inc.
ideas.thesis: Micron reports fiscal Q3 2026 earnings on June 24 after close, and this is the single most consequential semiconductor print of the quarter. The company itself guided to ~$33.5B revenue and ~81% gross margins — a historic high — underpinned by HBM (high-bandwidth memory) that is fully sold out through 2026 under binding contracts. Street consensus sits at $34.66B revenue and ~$19.95 EPS, with the bull case stretching to $40.9B, and 27 analysts at Strong Buy with zero Sell ratings. The key number to watch is gross margin: if it clears or holds 81%, it validates the structural HBM thesis; if it compresses, the cycle-risk bear case dominates.
ideas.catalyst: Fiscal Q3 2026 earnings report, Wednesday June 24, 2026 after market close — the first print that will test whether Micron's ~244% YTD rally rests on durable structural HBM pricing or a cyclical peak
ideas.edge: Analyst consensus Strong Buy (27/27 analysts, zero Sells); average 12-month price target ~$550–$1,091 range (wide disagreement reflects genuine bull/bear divide, not staleness); company-guided 81% gross margin would be far above Micron's historical norm; HBM sold out through 2026 on multi-year binding contracts per management — a visibility level rare in memory; options volatility flagged as elevated heading into print
ideas.bull_case: HBM4 allocations for NVIDIA's Vera Rubin platform confirmed on the call, gross margin holds at or above 81%, and management raises FY2027 guidance — re-rating the stock toward the $1,091–$1,500 bull-target cluster and cementing the 'structural franchise, not cycle peak' thesis.
ideas.bear_case: Revenue comes in near the low end of the $33.7B–$40.9B analyst range, gross margin disappoints versus 81% guidance (historically the first warning sign of a cycle turn), and Samsung/SK Hynix HBM4 ramp commentary introduces supply-side competition risk — stock at 244% YTD has zero margin for a miss.

ideas.name: FedEx Corp.
ideas.thesis: FedEx reports Q4 FY2026 earnings on Tuesday June 23, and this is the first quarter fully reflecting the company as a pure-play express/ground network after completing the FDXF freight spin-off on June 1. Analysts will get a cleaner read on the core FedEx margin and Network 2.0 cost-savings trajectory without freight noise. The US-Iran peace deal and falling oil prices provide a tailwind on fuel costs, a meaningful operating expense line. The question is whether the first clean post-spinoff quarter reveals a leaner, more profitable business or exposes that freight had been subsidizing parcel margins.
ideas.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings, Tuesday June 23, 2026 — first quarterly report post-FDXF spin-off (completed June 1, 2026), offering the market's first clean view of the standalone FedEx operating model
ideas.edge: Post-spinoff clarity event: market has not yet had a single clean-quarter earnings print of the new FedEx structure, creating price-discovery potential in either direction; falling jet fuel (heating oil proxy -3.5% on June 15) directly benefits express margins; prior Q3 showed adjusted operating margin of 6.7% — any expansion toward the 8%+ level that management's Network 2.0 targets would be a meaningful re-rating trigger
ideas.bull_case: Management confirms Network 2.0 savings are accelerating, Q4 adjusted margins beat on lower fuel and improved yields, and FY2027 guidance frames a sustained margin expansion path — the spinoff thesis is validated and FDX re-rates toward logistics peers trading at higher multiples.
ideas.bear_case: Q4 reveals that the FedEx core network is more margin-challenged than the blended pre-spinoff numbers suggested, volume softness from tariff-related trade headwinds weighs on international priority, and the first-quarter comps are ugly versus an elevated prior year — delivering a 'sell the news' event on the spinoff.

ideas.name: Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund
ideas.thesis: The Fed releases 2026 bank stress test results for 32 major US lenders on Wednesday June 24 at 4pm EDT. While the Fed pre-announced that this year's results will NOT directly reset capital requirements (those stay frozen until 2027 while Warsh's task forces review the framework), the results still matter: they provide the public capital-health scorecard and, if banks pass cleanly as expected, could unlock sentiment-driven buyback and dividend acceleration announcements from major lenders — a pattern that historically moves bank stocks in the 24-48 hours post-release. XLF provides broad exposure without single-name concentration risk.
ideas.catalyst: Fed 2026 bank stress test results, Wednesday June 24, 4pm EDT — 32 banks tested against a 10% unemployment / 30% home price decline / 39% CRE decline scenario; historically, clean results trigger dividend/buyback announcements within days
ideas.edge: The Fed pre-announced capital requirements remain unchanged through 2027, reducing downside surprise risk from the test itself; in the 2025 test all 22 banks remained well above minimums absorbing ~$550B in hypothetical losses — a similar outcome this year would be a green light for capital return announcements; XLF trades at a modest premium to book versus historical norms but not at extreme valuation, and the hawkish Fed backdrop (potential rate hike) structurally supports net interest margins for banks
ideas.bull_case: 32 banks pass cleanly, major lenders announce dividend increases and buyback expansions within 48 hours (as in 2025), and the concurrent May PCE print on June 25 shows inflation moderating slightly — giving banks a dual tailwind of capital return clarity plus a softer rate-hike timeline.
ideas.bear_case: One or more large banks surprise to the downside in the stress test (even without capital requirement consequences, a weak result is a headline risk), and the hawkish FOMC overhang keeps multiple expansion capped; if May PCE on June 25 comes in hotter than April's 3.80%, rate-hike fears could outweigh any buyback optimism.
